topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the shared dorm room had become a thick, suffocating blanket. Every time I walked in, a tremor would run through my hands, a nervous flutter beneath my skin. It started subtly—a misplaced book, a slightly rearranged desk—but then I saw it. My journal, usually tucked under my pillow, was open on the nightstand. The handwriting was undeniably mine, detailing anxieties about exams and vague fears about the future. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, and I froze, my breath catching in my throat. When my roommate, Alex, glanced up, their expression was one of casual curiosity, entirely lacking in recognition of the sudden, stark terror gripping me. I snatched the book, my movements jerky, and backed away toward the door, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird.

[story 2]

I kept glancing toward the door, my gaze darting between my laptop screen and the corner of my desk where my diary lay. A persistent prickling sensation crawled up my neck, making the fine hairs there stand up. It wasn't just the late hours; it was the deliberate way Alex lingered in the room, their presence somehow amplifying the quiet. Tonight, the air felt charged, brittle. I heard the soft rustle of paper, a sound so small it could have been the ventilation system, but it sounded too intentional. When I finally dared to look, my vision blurred slightly. Alex was sitting in their chair, illuminated by the desk lamp, a page turned slowly, their attention fixed on the ink spilling from my private thoughts. A wave of intense unease washed over me, so profound it made my palms slick with sweat. I stood up abruptly, the scrape of my chair echoing loudly in the sudden, cavernous quiet.

[story 3]

My focus was fractured. Every sound in the room—the hum of the mini-fridge, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, pressing in on my skin. I was constantly scanning the periphery, my muscles coiled tight, waiting for some disruption. It was the atmosphere itself that was wrong; heavy, expectant. Then, I caught the movement. It was a fleeting glimpse, a shadow passing near my side of the room, and I realized, with a sickening lurch in my chest, that Alex was reading my journal. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My entire body locked up; I couldn't move, couldn't scream. I just stared, my eyes wide and unblinking, the frantic thumping inside my chest the only sound in the room as I processed the invasion.
